body_txt: There's something about cruelty towards animals that really gets to a lot of people. Maybe it's the fact that there's so little many of them (especially ones smaller than humans) can do to defend themselves. Maybe it's that fact that they often don't even understand why a human is lashing out at them. Maybe it's the fact that it's often so gratuitous and unnecessary . Or maybe it's the fact that we instinctively feel protective over all things that can't speak up for themselves. Whatever the reason, almost all societies are going to look down on somebody if they hurt an animal for no good reason. Animals considered to be dangerous, such as crocodiles , snakes , bears , sharks , and big cats are usually an exception; see What Measure Is a Non-Cute? for a possible reason, though it may also have a lot to do with the fact that those animals aren't as defenseless and therefore are not seen as easy victims. Furthermore, they may be a threat to people and understandably force them to use violence. Even then they are still likely to be seen sympathetically under certain circumstances. Pets in particular are seen as defenseless, as small animals cannot do much to defend themselves beyond biting and scratching. As Real Life shows, however, there may be exceptions with people tranquilizing dangerous animals in order to toy with them. In this case, they are as defenseless as the harmless animals and the perpetrator is just as bad as the others and even more clearly a Dirty Coward . An almost surefire way to make a character irredeemable is to invoke this trope (especially when contrasted against a villain who does not abuse animals, because Even Evil Has Standards ). In addition, animal cruelty, especially torture and mutilation, is often treated as an early warning sign for future violent criminals such as a Serial Killer . Sometimes, however, the whole thing may be played for Black Comedy (and Cringe Comedy ) instead, in which case the show may take Refuge in Audacity and the person involved may not exactly be bad so much as they're stupid. Note that as a general rule, the abuse must be human-on-animal (or, in works where humans don't exist, sapient-on-nonsapient), except for situations involving Furry Confusion . The animals can be sapient, but not highly anthropomorphized . Being able to use a human language is acceptable, but the animals in the work must have roughly the same relationship with humans as they do in real life , so far as the humans concerned don't know about their sapience. The abuser doesn't even necessarily have to be a human — this trope also applies if an animal that is higher on the Sliding Scale of Anthropomorphism is abusing an animal that is lower on the Sliding Scale of Anthropomorphism . Kick the Dog and If You're So Evil, Eat This Kitten! are not always related, since any act of gratuitous evil besides hurting animals can be substituted there. See also Cruella to Animals and Exotic Entree for more specific variants, and Enemy to All Living Things for a more supernatural one. Chronic Pet Killer may also overlap if the killing isn't done on accident. Black Comedy Animal Cruelty , is when this trope is Played for Laughs . Diabolical Dog Catcher and Repulsive Ringmaster are specific character types who usually fall under this trope. Animal Lover is the direct inverse. While exaggerated in fiction, psychologists have even observed that animal abuse is a common early warning sign of a Serial Killer or Domestic Abuse . As noted on the same sources , this is especially true of children living in an abusive environment who may use an animal to reenact violent episodes from their life . Given help, many of these kids grow up to lead healthy and productive lives. Sub-Trope to Good Taming, Evil Taming . Compare Menagerie of Misery , in which bad people also own zoos and abuse animals. Contrast Consenting Animal Performers , where performing animals have human-level intelligence (and often, human speech capabilities), which makes it clear that they're performing because they want to. For in-depth information on the awful subject, see the article on Wikipedia . If this is a more fearful concept, then this may be related to zoophobia . Note when adding examples don't just mention a character abuses an animal, but mention how they're an animal abuser and how they mistreat other people. As a rule of thumb, your example will need to legally qualify as animal abuse in Real Life in order to count (e.g. a person kicking a chicken counts, but a person chopping a chicken's head off doesn't count). Also, just like Abusive Parents , this trope is not for complaining about characters you dislike. Since there is a lot of debate on what does and does not count as animal abuse, No Real Life Examples, Please! noreallife
